column5:
default='l2'

Specify the norm of the penalty:

- `None`: no penalty is added;
- `'l2'`: add a L2 penalty term and it is the default choice;
- `'l1'`: add a L1 penalty term;
- `'elasticnet'`: both L1 and L2 penalty terms are added.

.. warning::
Some penalties may not work with some solvers. See the parameter
`solver` below
column6:
to know the compatibility between the penalty and
solver.

.. versionadded:: 0.19
l1 penalty with SAGA solver (allowing 'multinomial' + L1)

.. deprecated:: 1.8
`penalty` was deprecated in version 1.8 and will be removed in 1.10.
Use `l1_ratio` instead. `l1_ratio=0` for `penalty='l2'`
column7:
`l1_ratio=1` for
`penalty='l1'` and `l1_ratio` set to any float between 0 and 1 for
`'penalty='elasticnet'`.\n"

column2:
default=1.0

Inverse of regularization strength; must be a positive float.
Like in support vector machines
column3:
smaller values specify stronger
regularization. `C=np.inf` results in unpenalized logistic regression.
For a visual example on the effect of tuning the `C` parameter
with an L1 penalty

with `0 <= l1_ratio <= 1`. Setting
`l1_ratio=1` gives a pure L1-penalty
column4:
setting `l1_ratio=0` a pure L2-penalty.
Any value between 0 and 1 gives an Elastic-Net penalty of the form
`l1_ratio * L1 + (1 - l1_ratio) * L2`.

.. warning::
Certain values of `l1_ratio`
column5: i.e. some penalties
column6:
may not work with some
solvers. See the parameter `solver` below
column7:
to know the compatibility between
the penalty and solver.

.. versionchanged:: 1.8
Default value changed from None to 0.0.

.. deprecated:: 1.8
`None` is deprecated and will be removed in version 1.10. Always use
`l1_ratio` to specify the penalty type.\n"

column3:
see also
:ref:`this equation `) formulation. Dual formulation
is only implemented for l2 penalty with liblinear solver. Prefer `dual=False`
when n_samples > n_features.\n"

column2:
default=True

Specifies if a constant (a.k.a. bias or intercept) should be
added to the decision function.\n"

default=1

Useful only when the solver `liblinear` is used
and `self.fit_intercept` is set to `True`. In this case
column5:
i.e. a \"synthetic\" feature with constant value equal to
`intercept_scaling` is appended to the instance vector.
The intercept becomes
``intercept_scaling * synthetic_feature_weight``.

.. note::
The synthetic feature weight is subject to L1 or L2
regularization as all other features.
To lessen the effect of regularization on synthetic feature weight
(and therefore on the intercept) `intercept_scaling` has to be increased.\n"

column2:
default=None

Weights associated with classes in the form ``{class_label: weight}``.
If not given
column3:
all classes are supposed to have weight one.

The \"balanced\" mode uses the values of y to automatically adjust
weights inversely proportional to class frequencies in the input data
as ``n_samples / (n_classes * np.bincount(y))``.

Note that these weights will be multiplied with sample_weight (passed
through the fit method) if sample_weight is specified.

.. versionadded:: 0.17
*class_weight='balanced'*\n"

column4:
'saga' or 'liblinear' to shuffle the
data. See :term:`Glossary ` for details.\n"

column5: 'sag'
column7:
default='lbfgs'

Algorithm to use in the optimization problem. Default is 'lbfgs'.
To choose a solver
column8:
you might want to consider the following aspects:

- 'lbfgs' is a good default solver because it works reasonably well for a wide
class of problems.
- For :term:`multiclass` problems (`n_classes >= 3`)
column9:
all solvers except
'liblinear' minimize the full multinomial loss
column10:
'liblinear' will raise an
error.
- 'newton-cholesky' is a good choice for
`n_samples` >> `n_features * n_classes`
column11:
especially with one-hot encoded
categorical features with rare categories. Be aware that the memory usage
of this solver has a quadratic dependency on `n_features * n_classes`
because it explicitly computes the full Hessian matrix.
- For small datasets
column13:
whereas 'sag'
and 'saga' are faster for large ones;
- 'liblinear' can only handle binary classification by default. To apply a
one-versus-rest scheme for the multiclass setting one can wrap it with the
:class:`~sklearn.multiclass.OneVsRestClassifier`.

.. warning::
The choice of the algorithm depends on the penalty chosen (`l1_ratio=0`
for L2-penalty
column14:
`l1_ratio=1` for L1-penalty and `0 < l1_ratio < 1` for
Elastic-Net) and on (multinomial) multiclass support:

================= ======================== ======================
solver l1_ratio multinomial multiclass
================= ======================== ======================
'lbfgs' l1_ratio=0 yes
'liblinear' l1_ratio=1 or l1_ratio=0 no
'newton-cg' l1_ratio=0 yes
'newton-cholesky' l1_ratio=0 yes
'sag' l1_ratio=0 yes
'saga' 0<=l1_ratio<=1 yes
================= ======================== ======================

.. note::
'sag' and 'saga' fast convergence is only guaranteed on features
with approximately the same scale. You can preprocess the data with
a scaler from :mod:`sklearn.preprocessing`.

.. seealso::
Refer to the :ref:`User Guide ` for more
information regarding :class:`LogisticRegression` and more specifically the
:ref:`Table `
summarizing solver/penalty supports.

.. versionadded:: 0.17
Stochastic Average Gradient (SAG) descent solver. Multinomial support in
version 0.18.
.. versionadded:: 0.19
SAGA solver.
.. versionchanged:: 0.22
The default solver changed from 'liblinear' to 'lbfgs' in 0.22.
.. versionadded:: 1.2
newton-cholesky solver. Multinomial support in version 1.6.\n"

Useless for liblinear solver. See :term:`the Glossary `.

column5:

:ref:`sphx_glr_auto_examples_svm_plot_svm_tie_breaking.py` for an
example of its usage with ``decision_function_shape='ovr'``.

Shannon information gain
column5: see :ref:`tree_mathematical_formulation`.\n"

column3:
default=\"best\"

The strategy used to choose the split at each node. Supported
strategies are \"best\" to choose the best split and \"random\" to choose
the best random split.\n"

column2:
default=None

The maximum depth of the tree. If None
column3:
then nodes are expanded until
all leaves are pure or until all leaves contain less than
min_samples_split samples.\n"

column2:
default=2

The minimum number of samples required to split an internal node:

- If int
column3:
then consider `min_samples_split` as the minimum number.
- If float
column4:
then `min_samples_split` is a fraction and
`ceil(min_samples_split * n_samples)` are the minimum
number of samples for each split.

.. versionchanged:: 0.18
Added float values for fractions.\n"

column2:
default=1

The minimum number of samples required to be at a leaf node.
A split point at any depth will only be considered if it leaves at
least ``min_samples_leaf`` training samples in each of the left and
right branches. This may have the effect of smoothing the model
column4:
then consider `min_samples_leaf` as the minimum number.
- If float
column5:
then `min_samples_leaf` is a fraction and
`ceil(min_samples_leaf * n_samples)` are the minimum
number of samples for each node.

Added float values for fractions.\n"

column2:
default=0.0

The minimum weighted fraction of the sum total of weights (of all
the input samples) required to be at a leaf node. Samples have
equal weight when sample_weight is not provided.\n"

column4:
default=None

The number of features to consider when looking for the best split:

- If int
column5:
then consider `max_features` features at each split.
- If float
column7:
int(max_features * n_features_in_))` features are considered at
each split.
- If \"sqrt\"
column10:
then `max_features=n_features`.

.. note::

The search for a split does not stop until at least one
valid partition of the node samples is found
column11:
even if it requires to
effectively inspect more than ``max_features`` features.\n"

column3:
default=None

Controls the randomness of the estimator. The features are always
randomly permuted at each split
column4:
even if ``splitter`` is set to
``\"best\"``. When ``max_features < n_features``
column5:
the algorithm will
select ``max_features`` at random at each split before finding the best
split among them. But the best found split may vary across different
runs
column6: even if ``max_features=n_features``. That is the case
column7:
if the
improvement of the criterion is identical for several splits and one
split has to be selected at random. To obtain a deterministic behaviour
during fitting
column8:
``random_state`` has to be fixed to an integer.
See :term:`Glossary ` for details.\n"

column2:
default=None

Grow a tree with ``max_leaf_nodes`` in best-first fashion.
Best nodes are defined as relative reduction in impurity.
If None then unlimited number of leaf nodes.\n"

default=0.0

A node will be split if this split induces a decrease of the impurity
greater than or equal to this value.

The weighted impurity decrease equation is the following::

N_t / N * (impurity - N_t_R / N_t * right_impurity
- N_t_L / N_t * left_impurity)

where ``N`` is the total number of samples
column3:
``N_t`` is the number of
samples at the current node
column4:
``N_t_L`` is the number of samples in the
left child
column5:
and ``N_t_R`` is the number of samples in the right child.

``N``
column7: ``N_t_R`` and ``N_t_L`` all refer to the weighted sum

column3:
default=None

Weights associated with classes in the form ``{class_label: weight}``.
If None
column4:
all classes are supposed to have weight one. For
multi-output problems
column5:
a list of dicts can be provided in the same
order as the columns of y.

Note that for multioutput (including multilabel) weights should be
defined for each class of every column in its own dict. For example
column6:
for four-class multilabel classification weights should be
[{0: 1
column16:
{4:1}].

The \"balanced\" mode uses the values of y to automatically adjust
weights inversely proportional to class frequencies in the input data
as ``n_samples / (n_classes * np.bincount(y))``

For multi-output
column17:
the weights of each column of y will be multiplied.

Note that these weights will be multiplied with sample_weight (passed
through the fit method) if sample_weight is specified.\n"

column2:
default=0.0

Complexity parameter used for Minimal Cost-Complexity Pruning. The
subtree with the largest cost complexity that is smaller than
``ccp_alpha`` will be chosen. By default
column3:
no pruning is performed. See
:ref:`minimal_cost_complexity_pruning` for details. See
:ref:`sphx_glr_auto_examples_tree_plot_cost_complexity_pruning.py`
for an example of such pruning.

.. versionadded:: 0.22\n"

column2:
default=None

Indicates the monotonicity constraint to enforce on each feature.
- 1: monotonic increase
- 0: no constraint
- -1: monotonic decrease

If monotonic_cst is None
column3:
no constraints are applied.

Monotonicity constraints are not supported for:
- multiclass classifications (i.e. when `n_classes > 2`)
column5:
- classifications trained on data with missing values.

The constraints hold over the probability of the positive class.

Read more in the :ref:`User Guide `.

.. versionadded:: 1.4\n"

The number of jobs to run in parallel. :meth:`fit`
column4:
:meth:`decision_path` and :meth:`apply` are all parallelized over the
trees. ``None`` means 1 unless in a :obj:`joblib.parallel_backend`
context. ``-1`` means using all processors. See :term:`Glossary
` for more details.\n"

column3:
default=None

Controls both the randomness of the bootstrapping of the samples used
when building trees (if ``bootstrap=True``) and the sampling of the
features to consider when looking for the best split at each node
(if ``max_features < n_features``).
See :term:`Glossary ` for details.\n"

column2:
default=0

Controls the verbosity when fitting and predicting.\n"

column3:
reuse the solution of the previous call to fit
and add more estimators to the ensemble
column5:
just fit a whole
new forest. See :term:`Glossary ` and
:ref:`tree_ensemble_warm_start` for details.\n"

column4:
default=None

Weights associated with classes in the form ``{class_label: weight}``.
If not given
column5:
all classes are supposed to have weight one. For
multi-output problems
column6:
a list of dicts can be provided in the same
order as the columns of y.

Note that for multioutput (including multilabel) weights should be
defined for each class of every column in its own dict. For example
column7:
for four-class multilabel classification weights should be
[{0: 1
column17:
{4:1}].

The \"balanced\" mode uses the values of y to automatically adjust
weights inversely proportional to class frequencies in the input data
as ``n_samples / (n_classes * np.bincount(y))``

The \"balanced_subsample\" mode is the same as \"balanced\" except that
weights are computed based on the bootstrap sample for every tree
grown.

For multi-output
column18:
the weights of each column of y will be multiplied.

Note that these weights will be multiplied with sample_weight (passed
through the fit method) if sample_weight is specified.\n"

column2:
default=0.0

Complexity parameter used for Minimal Cost-Complexity Pruning. The
subtree with the largest cost complexity that is smaller than
``ccp_alpha`` will be chosen. By default
column3:
no pruning is performed. See
:ref:`minimal_cost_complexity_pruning` for details. See
:ref:`sphx_glr_auto_examples_tree_plot_cost_complexity_pruning.py`
for an example of such pruning.

.. versionadded:: 0.22\n"

the number of samples to draw from X
to train each base estimator.

- If None (default)
column5:
then draw `max_samples` samples.
- If float

column2:
default=None

Indicates the monotonicity constraint to enforce on each feature.
- 1: monotonic increase
- 0: no constraint
- -1: monotonic decrease

If monotonic_cst is None
column3:
no constraints are applied.

Monotonicity constraints are not supported for:
- multiclass classifications (i.e. when `n_classes > 2`)
column5:
- classifications trained on data with missing values.

The constraints hold over the probability of the positive class.

Read more in the :ref:`User Guide `.

.. versionadded:: 1.4\n"

column2:
default=5

Number of neighbors to use by default for :meth:`kneighbors` queries.\n"

column4:
default='uniform'

Weight function used in prediction. Possible values:

- 'uniform' : uniform weights. All points in each neighborhood
are weighted equally.
- 'distance' : weight points by the inverse of their distance.
in this case
column5:
closer neighbors of a query point will have a
greater influence than neighbors which are further away.
- [callable] : a user-defined function which accepts an
array of distances
column6:
and returns an array of the same shape
containing the weights.

Refer to the example entitled
:ref:`sphx_glr_auto_examples_neighbors_plot_classification.py`
showing the impact of the `weights` parameter on the decision
boundary.\n"

column5:
default='auto'

Algorithm used to compute the nearest neighbors:

- 'ball_tree' will use :class:`BallTree`
- 'kd_tree' will use :class:`KDTree`
- 'brute' will use a brute-force search.
- 'auto' will attempt to decide the most appropriate algorithm
based on the values passed to :meth:`fit` method.

Note: fitting on sparse input will override the setting of
this parameter

column2:
default=30

Leaf size passed to BallTree or KDTree. This can affect the
speed of the construction and query
column3:
as well as the memory
required to store the tree. The optimal value depends on the
nature of the problem.\n"

column2:
default=2

Power parameter for the Minkowski metric. When p = 1
column4:
and euclidean_distance (l2) for p = 2.
For arbitrary p
column5:
minkowski_distance (l_p) is used. This parameter is expected
to be positive.\n"

column2:
default='minkowski'

Metric to use for distance computation. Default is \"minkowski\"
column3:
which
results in the standard Euclidean distance when p = 2. See the
documentation of `scipy.spatial.distance
`_ and
the metrics listed in
:class:`~sklearn.metrics.pairwise.distance_metrics` for valid metric
values.

If metric is \"precomputed\"
column4:
X is assumed to be a distance matrix and
must be square during fit. X may be a :term:`sparse graph`
column5:
in which
case only \"nonzero\" elements may be considered neighbors.

If metric is a callable function
column6:
it takes two arrays representing 1D
vectors as inputs and must return one value indicating the distance
between those vectors. This works for Scipy's metrics
column7:
but is less
efficient than passing the metric name as a string.\n"

column2:
default=None

Additional keyword arguments for the metric function.\n"

``None`` means 1 unless in a :obj:`joblib.parallel_backend` context.
``-1`` means using all processors. See :term:`Glossary `
for more details.
Doesn't affect :meth:`fit` method.\n"
